title: Your first if statement
instruction:
All the code you have written so far runs exactly the same way every time. But real programs need to behave differently depending on the situation.

`if` runs a block of code only when a condition is `True`.

```python
if condition:
    # this only runs if condition is True
```

Notice the colon `:` and the **indentation** — Python uses indentation (4 spaces) instead of curly braces.

Run this. Then change `score` to 40 and run again.
successMessage: When score is 85, the print runs. When it's 40, nothing runs. The condition controls everything.

title: else — the backup plan
instruction:
`else` runs when the `if` condition is `False`.

```python
if condition:
    # runs when True
else:
    # runs when False
```

Only one of the two blocks ever runs — never both.

Run this with `score = 85` (passes), then change to `score = 40` (fails).
successMessage: if/else — one path or the other. The condition decides which. Every decision in programming comes down to this.

title: elif — multiple options
instruction:
`elif` is short for *else if*. Use it to check multiple conditions in order.

Python checks top to bottom and runs the first block whose condition is `True`. The rest are skipped.

Add two more `elif` conditions: one for 70–79 (`grade = "C"`), one for 60–69 (`grade = "D"`).

codeChecks.message: Add: elif score >= 70:
Add: elif score >= 60:
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Four grades, one chain. elif checks conditions in order — the first True one wins. 78 correctly gives C. Clean and readable.

title: Comparison operators
instruction:
Comparisons produce `True` or `False`:

| Operator | Meaning |
|----------|----------|
| `==` | equal to |
| `!=` | not equal to |
| `>` | greater than |
| `<` | less than |
| `>=` | greater than or equal |
| `<=` | less than or equal |

Common mistake: `=` is assignment. `==` is comparison.

Run this and check all the outputs.
starter:
age = 18
print(age == 18)
print(age != 20)
print(age > 17)
print(age < 21)
print(age >= 18)
print(age <= 17)
successMessage: All comparisons return True or False. Note the last one: 18 <= 17 is False. The = sign in >= and <= does not mean assignment — it means 'or equal to'.

title: Nested if — decisions inside decisions
instruction:
An `if` block can contain another `if` inside it.

The inner `if` only runs if the outer condition is already `True`.

The indentation tells Python which block belongs where.

Add a nested check inside the `if is_logged_in:` block that also checks `is_premium`.
starter:
is_logged_in = True
is_premium = True

# TODO: if logged in, check if premium and print different messages
if is_logged_in:
    print("Welcome!")

codeChecks.message: Add a nested check: if is_premium:
Print a message that mentions premium.

successMessage: Nested if statements. The outer condition acts as a gate — the inner check only runs when you are already past the outer one.

title: Indentation is everything
instruction:
Python uses indentation to define blocks. Not curly braces — spaces.

The standard is **4 spaces** per level. Mixing tabs and spaces causes errors.

This code is broken — the `print` inside `else` has wrong indentation. Fix it.
hint: The print inside else needs to be indented 4 spaces.

codeChecks.message: Indent the print under else by 4 spaces.

successMessage: Indentation fixed. This is the single most common error for new Python programmers. Your editor helps — but watch for mixed tabs and spaces.

title: if on one line — ternary
instruction:
For simple if/else you can write everything on one line:

`value = "yes" if condition else "no"`

This is called a ternary expression. Use it when the logic is simple and fits clearly on one line.

Rewrite the four-line if/else below as a single ternary line.
starter:
age = 20

# Rewrite this as a ternary:
# if age >= 18:
#     status = "adult"
# else:
#     status = "minor"

# TODO: write the ternary here
print(status)

codeChecks.message: Use a ternary: status = "adult" if age >= 18 else "minor"
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: One line, same result. Ternary expressions are great for simple conditions — but don't nest them. Readability matters more than brevity.

title: pass — a placeholder block
instruction:
Python requires at least one statement inside every block. If you want an empty block (as a placeholder while building), use `pass`.

`pass` does nothing — it just satisfies Python's block requirement.

Add a `pass` to the empty `else` block below so it runs without error.

codeChecks.message: Add pass to the empty else block.
Replace the TODO comment with pass.

successMessage: pass is the intentional no-op. Use it as a placeholder when you know a block will do something later but is not ready yet.

title: match — Python's switch statement
instruction:
Python 3.10 added `match` — like a switch statement in other languages. Cleaner than long if/elif chains when matching exact values.

```python
match command:
    case "quit":
        ...
    case "help":
        ...
    case _:
        # default (like else)
```

Add two more cases to the match below: `"pause"` and `"resume"`.
starter:
command = "pause"

match command:
    case "start":
        print("Starting...")
    case "stop":
        print("Stopping...")
    # TODO: add pause and resume cases
    case _:
        print(f"Unknown command: {command}")

codeChecks.message: Add: case "pause":
Add: case "resume":
Replace the TODO comment with your cases.

successMessage: match is Python 3.10+. It is cleaner than a long if/elif chain when matching specific values. _ is the catch-all case (like else).

title: Fix all the bugs
instruction:
This program has four bugs:
1. Uses `=` instead of `==` in the comparison
2. Missing colon after `else`
3. Wrong indentation on the print inside else
4. Using lowercase `true` instead of `True`

Fix all four.
hint: ==, colon after else, 4-space indent, capital True
starter:
is_member = true

if is_member = True:
    print("Member discount applied.")
else
    print("No discount.")

codeChecks.message: Use capital True: is_member = True
Use == for comparison, not =
Add colon after else: else:

successMessage: Four bugs, all fixed. Memorise these: True not true, == not =, colon after every block header, 4-space indentation.

title: Build an age checker
instruction:
No starter code.

Create a variable `age`. Then write an if/elif/else chain that prints:
- `"Child"` if age < 13
- `"Teenager"` if age is 13–17
- `"Adult"` if age is 18–64
- `"Senior"` if age 65 or above
hint: Four cases — use elif for the middle ones.

codeChecks.message: Create an age variable.
Use elif for the middle cases.
Handle the Child case.
Handle the Senior case.

successMessage: Four categories, one clean if/elif/else chain. You now know how to make your programs smart. Chapter 5 done.
